title: Polynomial Methods in Learning and Statistics
speaker: Ankur Moitra
abstract: My goal in this talk is to survey some of the emerging applications of polynomial methods in both learning and in statistics. I will give two examples from my own work in which the solution to well-studied problems in learning and statistics can be best understood through the language of algebraic geometry.

proof_stages.label: Pearson moment formulation and learning goal
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up mixtures of two Gaussians, Pearson's empirical moment equations, the modern polynomial-time/sample-complexity learning question, and the result that the first six moments robustly support learning in one dimension.

proof_stages.label: Exact identifiability reduced to sign changes
proof_stages.summary: To show that six exact moments identify a two-Gaussian mixture up to relabeling, the speaker reduces the problem to proving that the signed difference of two distinct mixture densities has at most six zero crossings, since then a degree-six sign polynomial forces some moment difference to be nonzero.

proof_stages.label: Zero-crossing bound for linear combinations of Gaussians
proof_stages.summary: A proposition that any nonzero linear combination of k Gaussians has at most 2k-2 zero crossings is proved by induction using the Hummel-Gidas theorem that Gaussian convolution cannot increase zero crossings and the fact that convolution of Gaussians adds variances.

proof_stages.label: Robust six-moment conditioning and univariate algorithm
proof_stages.summary: The exact six-moment statement is recast as a polynomial system in the parameters, and a stability theorem asserts that sufficiently close first six moments imply componentwise-close parameters, yielding a sample-based method-of-moments algorithm.

proof_stages.label: Belkin-Sinha polynomial-family generalization
proof_stages.summary: For general polynomial families of distributions, Noetherian stabilization of moment-difference ideals supplies a finite identifying number of moments, while semialgebraic projection gives polynomial stability and hence a general, though ineffective, method-of-moments learning algorithm.

proof_stages.label: Nonnegative rank as polynomial feasibility
proof_stages.summary: After defining nonnegative rank and motivating nonnegative matrix factorization, the speaker expresses the question of rank at most r as a semialgebraic feasibility problem and identifies the exponential dependence on the number of variables in Renegar-style algorithms as the main bottleneck.

proof_stages.label: Full-rank variable reduction by pseudoinverses
proof_stages.summary: In the special case where the two factors have full column and row rank, pseudoinverses and basis changes reduce the feasibility formulation to linear and quadratic constraints in only 2r^2 transformation variables.

proof_stages.label: Rank-deficient reduction and complexity consequences
proof_stages.summary: For general factorizations, maximal independent subsets define finitely many local pseudoinverse transformations plus a polynomially encodable choice function, giving exact polynomial-time algorithms for fixed r, improved singly exponential dependence on r, and near-matching hardness under standard complexity assumptions.

examples.name: Pearson's Naples crab measurements
examples.note: The crab-measurement data illustrates how a visibly non-Gaussian empirical density led Pearson to posit a mixture of two Gaussians and motivates the moment-method setup, but the later proof does not depend on this data set.
examples.engagement_reason: used Pearson’s Naples crab data as historical motivation for Gaussian mixtures, without computing actual crab moments or parameters

passes.reason: Ordinary historical data set used to motivate Gaussian mixtures, not a structural classification exception or adversarial example.

examples.note: The heat-profile analogy makes the Hummel-Gidas zero-crossing theorem intuitive by identifying Gaussian convolution with diffusion, which smooths without creating new sign changes.

examples.name: Term-by-document matrix and topic modeling
examples.note: The information-retrieval matrix illustrates why nonnegative factorizations are useful: columns become distributions on words, factors become topics and document mixtures of topics.

examples.name: Politics, weather, and the missing word elephant in SVD
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker contrasts SVD with nonnegative factorization by noting that unrelated documents can appear correlated through jointly omitted words, illustrating the interpretability loss from negative coordinates.

examples.name: Cross-polytope-type obstruction to few simplex charts
examples.note: In an audience exchange, the speaker invokes a polytope with many facets to illustrate why many local linear transformations and a compatibility choice function may be needed in the rank-deficient nonnegative-rank algorithm.
examples.engagement_reason: uses the cross-polytope-type case to reason about needing many simplex/convex-hull charts and the resulting compatibility problem for choosing transformations
